content.text: When Claude invokes a tool, the arguments arrive as a plain Python

. Everything is parsed from JSON — including integers, booleans, and nested objects — so type coercion can happen silently. Using typed structures (Pydantic models or dataclasses) to validate and deserialise arguments before passing them to your tool implementation makes failures explicit, catches schema bugs early, and enables full static-type checking of tool code.

attrs.alt: Schema to raw input to validated Python types pipeline

content.text: to be the right type at runtime. Claude produces correct JSON most of the time, but JSON booleans, integers, and floats are ambiguous from Python's perspective, and optional fields may be absent. Validate before every call.
Model may pass a number as a string or vice versa
May arrive as float (42.0); int() cast needed
int 42 satisfies number; float() cast handles both
Model rarely confuses bool; safe from JSON
Single-item arrays may arrive as a bare string
Nested keys are always strings; values may need coercion

Using dataclasses as a Lightweight Alternative
If you prefer not to take a Pydantic dependency, Python's built-in
into a dataclass, including nested objects and optional fields:
The trade-off versus Pydantic: dacite does not run validators or apply constraints like
— it only checks types. Use it when you want structured access and type safety without constraint validation.
Tools sometimes need nested object parameters — for example, a filter with multiple fields, or an address with sub-fields. Define these as nested Pydantic models to get recursive validation:
Returning Structured Errors to the Model

When argument validation fails, return a structured error so the model can understand what went wrong and correct its next call. Plain error strings work but a structured format gives the model more actionable information:
Structured errors are especially valuable for tools with many parameters — the model sees exactly which field failed and why, enabling precise self-correction on the next turn.
Because argument validation happens independently of the Claude API, you can test it in pure unit tests without any API calls:

import pytest, json
from pydantic import ValidationError

def test_valid_weather_args():
    result = dispatch_tool("get_weather", {"city": "Tokyo", "units": "celsius"})
    data = json.loads(result)
    assert "error" not in data

def test_optional_units_defaults():
    # units omitted — should default to celsius without error
    result = dispatch_tool("get_weather", {"city": "Tokyo"})
    data = json.loads(result)
    assert "error" not in data

def test_missing_required_field():
    # city is required — omitting it should produce an error result
    result = dispatch_tool("get_weather", {})
    data = json.loads(result)
    assert data.get("error") == "invalid_arguments"
    assert any(e["field"] == "city" for e in data["details"])

def test_invalid_units_enum():
    result = dispatch_tool("get_weather", {"city": "Tokyo", "units": "kelvin"})
    data = json.loads(result)
    assert data.get("error") == "invalid_arguments"

def test_unknown_tool():
    result = dispatch_tool("nonexistent_tool", {})
    data = json.loads(result)
    assert data.get("error") == "unknown_tool"
JSON Schema types map to Python types predictably but JSON integers and numbers overlap — use
Optional arguments may be absent entirely — always supply defaults via Pydantic or
, never use direct key access on optional fields.
A typed dispatch table (model + handler per tool) centralises validation and makes adding new tools a one-line change.
